speaker: interviewer
text: My great-uncle is from the-- I see. --Saxen area. And now I was trying to ask when he left, but he left before my-- February the 22nd, 1995. The survivor is Gabby Silten; Dana Schwartz, interviewer; in Los Angeles, USA. And it's in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gabriele, I'd like to start by having you tell me your full name and where and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name at birth was Ruth Gabriele Silten. I was born in Berlin, in Germany, in 1933. My full name nowadays is Ruth Gabriele Sarah, because I added one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely. Please begin by telling us about your family as far back as you know, and what you know about them, and then lead up to your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I'll begin with the grandparents. I know a little further than that, but it'll bring us too far afield. Both of my grandfathers were born in Konigsberg, which was then, Prussia. I think it's now Russia. I'm not sure. But then it was Prussia, part of Germany. Both my grandmothers were born in Berlin. My father's father studied pharmacy, got a doctorate somewhere in 1890, was apprenticed in a number of pharmacies, at-- as was then the custom-- and in some way met his future wife in Berlin, after he had settled in Berlin. He bought a pharmacy in about-- let me see-- 1880 or thereabouts. His pharmacy is just over 100 years old now; married my grandmother in 1900; and in due time had two sons, born in 1901 and 1904. The second was my father, Fritz. And they-- they don't have any middle names. The other grandfather, my mother's father, did all sorts of different things. I'm not entirely sure what, but I do know that when I was born he ran a dry-cleaning establishment. He went broke a number of times. He made some money a number of times. But the last thing I know about is the dry-cleaning establishment. He also met my other grandmother in Berlin. They married. They also had two children-- my mother who's the oldest, Ilse, and her sister. Actually, there were-- there was one more, but she died at age eight. So both families had the two children. My parents met, again, in Berlin, of course, by some manipulation of my father's mother. Apparently, the two grandmothers knew each other somewhat, and by some manipulation, the two-- my two parents were brought together, and went out together for a while. And then my father was sent on an around-the-world trip in 1929 by his father. He worked for his father-- was co-owner of the pharmacy by then. And somewhere from in the middle of China, he telegraphed my mother-- would she marry him. And since my mother had learned from the telegraph office that 10 words cost as much as one, she telegraphed back, yes, 10 times. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's one of the earliest stories I heard. So they got married in 1931, and-- in August. And a couple of weeks before that, my mother's father died, and the wedding was not postponed. It wasn't possible, I suppose, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they stayed in Berlin. They got their own apartment, rented, of course, as again was then the custom. And I was born in '33-- in May '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you just take a few minutes to-- to tell us about the-- the relatives you know of that were further than your grandparents-- just to let us have an insight, which is fairly rare to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I know of a whole lot of them, actually. I know about my grandfather's parents, who are buried in Weissensee, in Berlin. I know where the graves are. My grandfather's buried there. And I know, actually, somewhat vaguely, but I can look it up, in what my father used to call the family archives, all the way back to 1700 or so on both sides of the family. In fact, it turns out, when you look at the family archives and the family trees, that my father's family and my mother's family are connected three times. The third time is my parents. But before that, also, there were marriages between those two families.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Where are--

speaker: interviewee
text: So.

speaker: interviewer
text: --those archives?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the moment they're in my garage. And it's three big boxes full of partly photographs, partly letters, family trees, diplomas. Like, my-- for example, my grandfather's doctoral diploma, childhood parents of my-- of pictures of my parents, [LAUGHS] and so on-- all these various things-- report cards from their school.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the ones--

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth--

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the seven-- the 18th and the 19th century?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's less there. There are pictures where pictures were made. I have a picture of my great-great-grandparents with 8 of their 14 children-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I found in those archives. It looks like a tintype.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it made a good picture. So it goes really way back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you going to-- where are you going to leave these archives?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the long run, eventually, my will states that they are to go to the US Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where they belong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK. Thank you. Then, let's begin with your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in 1933. I suppose if my parents had known what was going to happen, I wouldn't be sitting here. But they didn't, so here I am. And as far as I remember, nothing much really happened in my first five years, which is the years I passed in Berlin-- '33 to '38. We went to see my grandparents. I remember going there and playing with various things in the house. I remember being taken on walks by my grandmother with her dog, and being very jealous because the dog was allowed to run free, and I wasn't. [LAUGHS] Pretty much normal things. But in '38, my father left before my birthday. I didn't know then, of course, that he'd gone to Holland, but that's where he did go. And we followed him in September of '38. And again, I didn't know that we were going to go permanently. I only knew that we left behind what was left of my family-- my grandparents, of course; various cousins of my parents who had not yet left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My uncle, whom I knew, my father's brother, but who had already left; friends and so on, all of that was left behind. My parents, of course, left behind everything they owned. That did not register with me either, really-- not at that time. I remember part of the trip. I remember the train. I remember a boat, although why we should've taken a boat between Germany and Holland, I don't know, But we did. And we arrived in Amsterdam and stayed in a boarding house for a while, not having, as yet, an apartment. And that time I remember quite well, because I was left with a landlady by myself. I was five years old-- didn't speak Dutch. The landlady didn't speak German. She was supposed to feed me, and my mother went off, of course, to look for apartments. My father was off working. So I remember that time very well, because I was very unhappy. Nobody explained everything-- anything. Nobody said anything. So I was very unhappy. Eventually, we did find an apartment in the address I gave you in that sheet of paper, in what is now called the [NON-ENGLISH]. Then it was called the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the northern Amstel Lane, to translate, because the area was named after rivers in general. And since we were, once again, all three together, I began to be a bit happier. I was sent to-- I guess it would be called kindergarten now-- together with my upstairs neighbor, a girl of my own age. She's six months younger. And we got along well once we had learned each other's language. There again, this was a Montessori kindergarten, and I was immature for my age-- so my parents told me. I don't know that, obviously. And I really didn't do terribly well there. So I did not go on, and would not have gone on in any event, to any other Montessori school. In '39, when I turned six, I went to first grade. By then, we had settled in pretty much. I spoke Dutch by that time. My parents were still learning. My first-grade teacher was their tutor, but I had to translate off and on-- when we went to-- well, what's now called the market-- to the baker or the green grocer or whatever. But they learned fairly fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things would have been kind of settling down, except that in 1939, my grandmother came and joined us-- my father's mother. She got out of Germany somehow, and she left behind, of course, her husband, all her possessions, and everything, in addition to which she moved in with us. So we had two women trying to run one household. Mother-in-law, daughter-in-law, didn't get along too terribly well anyway. Everybody was nervous because of the impending war, or at least the unrest that was everywhere-- to the extent that even I noticed that something was wrong. I didn't know what was wrong, but obviously, my adults were not quite themselves. So my grandmother moved in. And she learned Dutch, actually, faster than my parents did. And I don't really know what she did during the day, as I went to school at that point. I have no idea what she did besides helping in the household, what there was of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was an apartment of what they then called four rooms-- two bedrooms, living room, dining room. And the dining room became my grandmother's bedroom as well, so I mean there wasn't that much to do. She made some friends, helped me with homework, drew for me-- she was very talented in drawing-- made doll clothes for me, and clothes for me as well. And as far as I know, that's about it. But there I could be mistaken. And then, in 1940, the war, as war actually started, because that's when the German army invaded Holland, the night of May 9 to May 10. So then the next morning, we were officially at war. And my father tried to explain to me what that meant. And I was to turn seven just a few days later, and naturally, he didn't get very far. But he tried. And again, in the beginning, nothing much changed. The Germans had all sorts of rules and regulations, which were published in the-- what the Dutch called the The Dutch government had fled to England and Canada, so the Germans had taken over. But most of those rules and regulations didn't concern me, because I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example, there was a curfew. People had to be home by 8:00. Well, I had to be in bed by [LAUGHS] 8 o'clock when and the light was out. What did I care everybody else had to be home by 8:00?

speaker: interviewee
text: And nothing concerned me specifically until prohibitions came, that we weren't allowed in the parks anymore, or to sit on the park benches, or to go to swimming pools, or to the theater, or whatever. I didn't go to the theater all that much, obviously. But these various places, we could not go. There were prohibitions about what we could not buy. We weren't allowed to have meat, or milk, or fruit. And obviously, yes, I noticed. And that was, I think, also the time that-- since I noticed it, my parents didn't say very much. I didn't say very much. And as a family, we became rather non-communicative altogether. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When they did explain things, they really-- I suppose they thought they explained it well, but they really didn't. For example, when we had to wear the yellow star, the Star of David-- well, obviously, some explanation was needed. So my father sat me down and try to explain now why, specifically, we had to wear this. First of all, I didn't know we were Jewish. We were non-observant. We didn't go to synagogue. We weren't member of any kind of organization. So I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were Jewish. OK, what does that mean? Well, in my family, it didn't mean anything. But here we were. I had to wear this star, which said Jew on it. And my [NON-ENGLISH]-- father tried to explain that this was a badge of pride. Now, it's very difficult to be proud of something if you don't know what the something is or what it means.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, by then I was about seven, and I knew enough that when people asked me, to say, oh, yes, I'm very proud of this. And our upstairs neighbor, Carla's mother-- Carla is the name of the little girl upstairs-- her mother, she thought that was so fantastic that I was supposed to come upstairs every day before school. And she would give me candy, which she did. Of course, I didn't mind that at all, needless to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there an explanation was needed and given, but it didn't do very much. For one thing, as I said, I didn't know what a Jew was. For another thing, to explain a Star of David-- who's David? You know, I hadn't the foggiest idea. David-- why not my father's name? Why not anybody's name? Why David? But that wasn't explained. I suppose my father really didn't know, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: About that time, also, segregation was made between Jewish schools and other schools. And the Nazis said, Jewish children go to Jewish schools only. Now in and of itself, that wasn't a problem, except, of course, that those schools who were designated as Jewish had to get rid of their non-Jewish children. So there was a big crossover in all directions, and everything was upset again. You lost your friends again. You didn't know where they went, or whether you'd ever see them again, because they might live somewhere else. And all these new people came in-- new children. So it was a major upset. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The school was close to home-- the elementary school. So I walked. And of course, I went alone. It was not the habit at that point that parents took children to school. When you were six year old and you went to first grade, you were plenty old enough to go by yourself. And so I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, pretty soon, there were a number of anxieties that came. Would I meet soldiers? And if so, if they talk to me, do I indicate that I speak German? Or do I pretend I speak only Dutch? If I speak German, what are they going to do with me? Are they going to take me? And where? And when? And how? And who's gonna know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's probably more thought out now than it was then. But it was there, especially since, very often, in crossing the street, we had to wait until these long columns of soldiers passed by. And in school, we were taught that soldiers could come in any time and inspect all these rules and regulations that I talked about earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: They could ask us, how did the fruit taste last night? Of course, we weren't supposed to have any. So whatever we had, we were supposed to say, we didn't have any. We're not supposed to have any.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called lying. And of course, at home, we were all taught that lying was practically a sin. You don't lie. It's not nice. And at school, we were taught that lying was definitely the thing to do. So we had to keep that apart. And I'm saying, we advisedly, because the other children had the same problems. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were other things that happened in those first, say, three war years. Most-- one of the-- really, most disconcerting things, I think, was the change in teachers. They had started deporting people by then, and every once in a while, your teacher would be deported. And from one day to the next, here was this new person. And new adjustments had to be made. So that was one. The teacher I remember best, possibly because we had her longest, had the habit of, when children were not in class, to send out a friend of that child to the child's home to find out what happened-- whether the child had the flu, or was sick, or what. And so I was sent out to my friend Peggy's home. She was a school friend. And there were several difficulties attached to that, because we couldn't go, of course, unless it was in school time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Holland, in those days, if a child was seen in the street between 9:00 and 12:00, school hours, or between 2:00 and 4:00, afternoon school hours, any adult, whether they knew you or not, felt absolutely justified in saying, hey what are you doing on the street? Why aren't you in school? So that was one thing to be avoided. Soldiers were the second thing to be avoided.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got to Peggy's house, I rang the bell, and of course, no answer. So eventually, a neighbor lady came out, asked the infamous question about why I wasn't in school. And after I'd explained that and told her what I wanted, she said, oh, well. They're gone. They came to take them away. And that was awful. I had lost a friend by then-- my friend Max, who lived kitty-corner from me in the same street; parents knew each other. We played over at each other's houses. And the reason we became friends is because neither of us were allowed to sing in first grade. And in order to keep an eye on us, we were both put in the very first row, the very first bench. And it was highly unusual to have mixed pairs in those days. So we became fast friends. He survived, incidentally. He lives in Baltimore nowadays. But he disappeared from one day to the next-- just like that. And in fact, I never did find out what happened to him until about eight years or so ago when he found me. Until then, I thought he was dead. He thought I was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Peggy also survived, as it turns out. It turns out, as I know from postwar times, that they had not been taken away. They'd gone into hiding, and she did survive. We lost touch eventually, though. So all these things, of course, were nerve-wracking for the adults, as well as for me. Blackout at the evening was nerve-wracking, because the house was totally, pitch black. Once the black paper was up in front of the windows, you couldn't see anything at all when the lights were out. And I was afraid of the dark, at best. Under the circumstances, I figured, if it's that dark, who knows who's hiding. So there were all sorts of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, the Nazis came a number of times during the night to take us away. We got to go back home, but they also took us away. One time, we went out to a restaurant, where my grandmother worked, in order to just get away for the night, and sat up all night peeling potatoes. That was, apparently at that time, a safe occupation. If you peeled potatoes, then for the time being you didn't go on transport. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back home the next day. And that was scary, because at night, of course, we weren't supposed to be out. So any time you heard a noise, you know, everybody went like this. And it was quite impossible. But we went back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they came another time, and they took us to what was then nicknamed the Jewish theater, which had actually been the-- the Dutch National Theater. But it was a place where Jews were concentrated before being sent on to whatever concentration camp. And so we were sent there, and for some reason let go again the next morning. And that was my first experience of a lot of people-- a lot of people-- in one room; with all the things that go together with that-- with children wailing and running around, and women crying, and men quarreling, and everybody trying to find out what was what-- nobody being able to find out. And eventually, in 1943, we were, in fact, picked up, as we called it then, and deported to Westerbork, which is a concentration camp in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got there by cattle train. But before we ever got to the train-- it took all day. The-- the day was a Sunday. It was the 20th of June. And for Holland, it was a really heat wave. Now that the idea was that we were allowed to have a backpack, a rucksack, and another bag that was worn crosswise like this. And it was called, in those days, a bread sack. And I suppose it was for food, although we put other things in it as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: My doll, for one, some doll clothes, shoes-- anything that wouldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your teddy bear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't take my teddy bear with me. My teddy bear, whose name is Brunette-- and her name is Brunette, because that is the name of the baby bear in the story, which in Holland, thus-- in which, in Holland, the three bears have names. So she's named after the baby bear. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't take this bear with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't take her. No, I took my doll, Peter, because I didn't know where we were going. By then, we knew that if you were deported, it was going to be bad. And I didn't want anything bad to happen to Brunette. So she went into hiding with my upstairs neighbor, Carla-- she and a little squirrel. But Brunette was the more important one, because she was, literally, everything to me. I could talk to her. And in my mind, of course, she-- she answered me. And I could tell her all my little anxieties-- which were big anxieties, of course, at the time-- that I couldn't talk to-- to my parents about or to friends. Carla and I talked quite a bit, too, but mostly I talked to Brunette. She always looks, as she still does-- now she's a little the worse for wear. She always had this yellow ribbon in her ear, not having enough hair. And she always had a little outfit like this. This one is a copy, but the original one looked exactly like my grandmother made it for her. Unfortunately, after about 40 years, it's rotting, literally. So I have it wrapped in paper and had a copy made.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Brunette went into hiding. And it gave me the feeling that if nothing happened to her, then really nothing could happen to me. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and she was safe. So, kind of, I was safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Peter survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter survived. Yes. I don't have him anymore, but he did survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was smaller than Brunette, probably about so big, and he had a cloth body. And he had-- as again was the custom in celluloid-- hands, and arms, and legs, and feet, and a head. He didn't have real hair. He had painted-on hair. And he had brown eyes and a smile on his face. And like Brunette, he had a green outfit. I don't know why. My grandmother made both of them. She must have liked green, is all I can say. [LAUGHS] And he had a pair of little pants. And he had a sweater. And he had a beret that he wore like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he give you a lot of comfort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Since I didn't have Brunette anymore, the bear definitely gave me-- I mean the doll gave me a lot of comfort, because she took-- he took the place of Brunette as far as confidences and so were concerned. And he made himself very useful, because the green outfit, for example, was wool and was eventually unraveled so that we could use that wool for mending stockings and other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whatever other clothes, doll clothes, I had taken, cotton type, I eventually bartered away-- way-- way much later, when we were already in Theresienstadt, for woollen clothes, for Peter, which in due time were unraveled and used for other things. So he made himself very useful, besides being my confidant and my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the trip to Westerbork take?

speaker: interviewee
text: In hindsight, it must have taken a good 8 or 10 hours. Now that means very little, except if you know that today, from Amsterdam to Westerbork, whether you go by train or by a car, it takes about 1 hour and a 1/2 or 2 hours-- no more, for sure. But we got into those trains by about noon, the hottest time of the day. And with all of our clothes on, various layers, and all the stuff that we carried, we were pretty hot. And we didn't get there until well after dark. Now in summer, in Holland, it doesn't get dark until about 11:00 at night. And I remember that it was dark. And they had these great big lamps shining on the gate. So--
